blockers.issue: Boundary-drop at line 193 discards EVERY event whose updated timestamp equals lastSeen, not just the previously-emitted high-water row. If two distinct events share the same millisecond `updated` (bulk import, recurring expansion, Google's coarse ms stamping), the second one is silently lost on every subsequent poll because the high-water never advances past it (highWater stays at that value and the next poll re-drops it). Spec intent is to dedupe the single boundary row, not collapse a timestamp bucket.
blockers.fix: Track the set of emitted event ids at the boundary timestamp in state (e.g. `last_seen_updated` + `last_seen_event_ids: string[]`). Drop an event only if `event.updated === lastSeen && last_seen_event_ids.includes(event.id)`. Persist the ids of all events emitted at the new highWater timestamp into nextState.

nits: Missing defensive recovery when `lastState.last_seen_updated` is absent/non-string — adapter falls through to an unbounded fetch with no `updated_min`, mirroring gmail.ts's `recoverFromTooOld` would harden against corrupted state rows.

`single_events:true` makes recurring-instance events almost never satisfy `created === updated`, so they'll always tag as 'updated' even on first emission; acceptable per spec literal wording but worth a code comment.
reviewer: opus-4.7 adversarial pass via Agent tool
